name: BAE Systems
overview: BAE Systems is Britain's largest defence company and one of the world's biggest, making combat aircraft, warships, armoured vehicles, electronics and cyber/financial-crime software. Its AI and autonomy work runs through FalconWorks (its air-domain research division), the GCAP/Tempest next-generation fighter programme, uncrewed 'collaborative' aircraft meant to fly alongside crewed jets, and machine-learning tools for cyber defence and fraud detection. Since 2024-2025 it has leaned heavily on partnerships - investing in AI start-ups and teaming with Lockheed Martin and Turkish Aerospace on autonomous drones.

established.label: BAE's answer to Skunk Works
established.plain: In July 2023 BAE launched FalconWorks, a research division inside its air business aimed at quickly developing future aircraft and air technology. It explicitly focuses on autonomy, artificial intelligence, quantum sensing, robotics and electric propulsion. Unlike its secretive US equivalents (Lockheed's Skunk Works, Boeing's Phantom Works), BAE pitches it as more open, working with universities and small companies.

established.claim: Tempest / GCAP fighter built on a digital twin
established.label: Designing a jet inside a computer first
established.plain: For the Tempest next-generation fighter (now part of the trilateral UK-Italy-Japan GCAP programme), BAE designs, tests and supports the aircraft using a 'digital twin' - a detailed computer model of every system and structure - plus model-based engineering and simulation, so problems are caught virtually before metal is cut. Test pilots have flown the aircraft in ground simulators long before a real one exists, and BAE says the jet's mission management will be AI-enhanced.

established.label: An AI 'wingman' drone for fast jets
established.plain: In February 2024 at the World Defence Show, BAE revealed a low-observable, roughly Hawk-sized uncrewed aircraft it plans to fly within about two years. It is designed to work alongside crewed jets like the Typhoon on electronic-attack, surveillance and strike missions, and would use AI for what BAE calls 'goal-based autonomy' - being given an objective rather than step-by-step instructions.

established.claim: NetReveal machine-learning fraud and money-laundering detection
established.label: AI hunting financial crime
established.plain: BAE's NetReveal platform used machine learning, behavioural profiling and anomaly detection to spot fraud and money-laundering in banks' transaction data, cutting false alarms and speeding up processing. This was a long-standing real-world AI product, not a concept. In 2022 BAE agreed to sell the NetReveal financial-crime business to AI software firm SymphonyAI, so it now sits outside BAE.

inProgress.claim: Lockheed Martin Skunk Works + FalconWorks autonomous air system
inProgress.label: Two giants building cheap robot jets
inProgress.plain: In September 2025 Lockheed Martin's Skunk Works and BAE's FalconWorks announced they would jointly develop a family of uncrewed, modular autonomous aircraft that can be launched many ways (air-dropped, from the ground, from ships or from large transport planes). The first version targets electronic warfare and attack, meant to protect crewed combat jets by jamming and disrupting enemy defences.

inProgress.claim: Oxford Dynamics investment and Prophesea AI integration
inProgress.label: Buying into a UK AI start-up
inProgress.plain: In August 2025 BAE took a strategic equity stake in Oxford Dynamics, a UK deep-tech start-up (founded 2020) that builds AI-driven autonomous systems and 'agentic AI' for interpreting data and making real-time decisions. The first job is to embed Oxford Dynamics' AI into BAE's Prophesea platform, software that helps defence customers keep assets like warships, vehicles and aircraft mission-ready.

inProgress.label: Scouting AI and autonomy start-ups
inProgress.plain: In 2025 BAE became a founding corporate partner of the MassChallenge UK accelerator, extending a US relationship of more than seven years. The aim is to find and work with early-stage start-ups in AI, autonomy, robotics and software-defined defence, giving them access to BAE's technology-scouting teams for possible pilots and partnerships.

latest.claim: BAE Systems + Turkish Aerospace (TAI) drone MOU
latest.label: Teaming on uncrewed aircraft sales
latest.plain: In November 2025 BAE and Turkish Aerospace Industries signed a memorandum of understanding to explore working together on uncrewed air systems (fixed-wing, rotary and hybrid). TAI brings its drone product range and manufacturing; BAE brings sales and customer reach across Europe and beyond. It is an early-stage agreement to pursue opportunities, not yet a specific aircraft contract.

suppliers.name: Lockheed Martin (Skunk Works)
suppliers.claim: Skunk Works says the BAE collaboration will deliver a 'rapidly deployable and modular' uncrewed system, initially for electronic warfare and attack to enhance the survivability of crewed combat aircraft.

suppliers.name: Oxford Dynamics
suppliers.claim: Oxford Dynamics describes itself as a UK deep-tech firm building intelligent autonomous and agentic-AI systems that interpret data and make real-time decisions for defence, security and space.

suppliers.name: MassChallenge UK
suppliers.claim: MassChallenge says its UK accelerator, with BAE as a founding partner, scales breakthrough start-ups in AI, autonomy, perceptual intelligence, robotics and data-centric engineering.

suppliers.name: Turkish Aerospace Industries (TAI)
suppliers.claim: TAI signed an MOU with BAE to explore collaboration across uncrewed air systems, contributing its UAV portfolio and manufacturing while BAE supports regional sales.

sources.name: Lockheed Martin - Skunk Works / FalconWorks collaboration

unknowns: Much of BAE's military AI work (the autonomy 'brain' inside Tempest/ACP, ISR and threat-detection algorithms) is classified, so public detail is thin and often marketing-framed. Where contract values or exact in-service dates were not clearly sourced, they are omitted here.
